pattern: Splitting the count by exactly how many of the stages end up occupied turns the problem into choosing which stages are used, counting the surjective assignments onto exactly that many, and multiplying by every band's independent score choice.
hints: Group every possible outcome by how many of the stages actually end up with at least one performer -- for a fixed count of occupied stages, the number of scoring choices is simply that count raised to the power of the score range.

For a fixed number of occupied stages, choosing which specific stages are used and then counting the onto-assignments of performers to exactly those stages is a classic inclusion-exclusion over the number of stages deliberately left empty.

Summing the product of (ways to choose which stages are used) times (surjective assignments onto them) times (score choices raised to that count) over every possible occupied-stage count from 1 up to the smaller of performers and stages gives the total, all reduced modulo one billion and seven.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, one performer with a choice of stage and score
statement example, several performers with a single possible score
statement example, equal performers and stages with a wider score range
only one stage available for many performers
far more stages than performers, so most stages stay empty
score range of exactly one, so score choice never differentiates outcomes

stress.description: random moderate n, x, y across the full allowed range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    hi = max(1, min(size, 1000))
    n = rng.randint(1, hi)
    x = rng.randint(1, hi)
    y = rng.randint(1, hi)
    return [n, x, y]
